summary: Hypothesis: account-scoped REST objects may be vulnerable to BOLA/IDOR if object references from one user can be queried with another valid token.

evidence.request:
GET /accounts/ HTTP/2
Host: api.robinhood.com
User-Agent: curl/8.x
Accept: */*
evidence.response: Observed in artifact: status 401 unauthenticated (endpoint exists and is protected, but ownership checks are unverified).

repro_steps: Authenticate two owned test accounts (A and B) in-scope.
Use account A to list account-owned objects (accounts/orders/positions/watchlists/documents) and collect object URLs/IDs.
Replay direct-object requests with account B token to the same object URLs/IDs.
Record whether access is correctly denied vs unexpectedly allowed for read/write actions.
impact_statement: If object-level authorization is missing on any account-owned endpoint, one customer could read or modify another customer’s brokerage data/actions.

summary: Hypothesis: notification device endpoint may rely on gateway metadata mapping and could be susceptible to header/metadata auth confusion.

evidence.request:
GET /notifications/devices/ HTTP/2
Host: api.robinhood.com
Accept: application/json
evidence.response: HTTP/2 403 with body: {"code":7,"message":"the \"Authorization\" key does not exist in metadata"}.

repro_steps: Send baseline authenticated request with standard Authorization header and confirm expected behavior.
Test canonicalization/duplication variants for Authorization and forwarding headers through the same edge path.
Compare responses and verify whether unauthenticated actor can induce authenticated context or access foreign device records.
Stop immediately and report if any cross-account device data is returned.
impact_statement: An auth-context confusion bug here could expose or modify push-device bindings, enabling account notification interception or disruption.

summary: Hypothesis: X1 GraphQL edge may have cross-environment identity-header confusion (prod vs staging identity headers) affecting authorization boundaries.

evidence.request:
POST /graphql HTTP/2
Host: api.vault.x1creditcard.com
Content-Type: application/json
Body: {"query":"{__typename}"}
evidence.response: HTTP/2 400; response headers expose accepted auth-related headers and include Access-Control-Allow-Headers with Authorization + X-Thrive-* identity headers, plus Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on vault edges.

repro_steps: Using only owned X1 test identities, obtain valid prod and staging identity contexts.
Attempt controlled matrix tests across api.x1creditcard.com, api.vault.x1creditcard.com, and api-staging.vault.x1creditcard.com using mismatched identity header families.
Check whether tokens/identity headers from one environment are accepted in another.
Verify object-level responses remain scoped to the caller account only.
impact_statement: If environment or identity-header boundaries are weak, an attacker might replay credentials across trust zones and access unauthorized card/account data.

summary: Hypothesis: order lifecycle endpoints may be vulnerable to sequencing/race-condition abuse (cancel/replace/fill timing) across brokerage order APIs.

evidence.request: Recon evidence only: host discovered as high-priority trading surface (no exploitation attempted).
evidence.response: orders/ks-order-api hosts appear in priority and in-scope lists; no differential state-transition testing yet.

repro_steps: With owned funded test account(s), place low-risk test orders permitted by program rules.
Run parallel cancel/replace/modify attempts near state transitions.
Check for inconsistent final states, duplicate execution, or stale authorization of prior state tokens.
Document deterministic replay and financial side effects before reporting.
impact_statement: Order-state race bugs can cause unauthorized executions or inconsistent balances/positions with direct financial impact.

summary: Deterministic unauthenticated 500 on OAuth revoke endpoint when using form-encoded token payload, while equivalent JSON payload returns controlled 400.

evidence.request:
POST /oauth2/revoke_token/ HTTP/2
Host: api.robinhood.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Body: token=11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111
evidence.response: HTTP/2 500 with generic server error HTML; reproducible 3/3. Control requests: same endpoint with JSON token -> 400, and POST /oauth2/token/ form payload -> 401.

repro_steps: Send unauthenticated POST to /oauth2/revoke_token/ with Content-Type application/x-www-form-urlencoded and body token=<uuid-like-value>.
Observe HTTP 500 response and stable body hash across repeated attempts (>=3).
Send comparison POST to same endpoint with JSON body {"token":"<same-value>"} and observe controlled 400 instead of 500.
Send control POST to /oauth2/token/ with form payload and observe non-5xx (401) to show endpoint-specific behavior.
impact_statement: Unauthenticated deterministic 500 on a security-sensitive OAuth endpoint suggests parser/auth-order weakness and creates error-path availability risk; may indicate deeper revoke-flow validation flaws.
next_validation: Verify whether malformed form payload can produce sustained elevated error rate (low-rate safe check first).
Retest with valid owned authenticated OAuth context to assess whether bug affects real token revocation logic or only pre-auth parser path.
Capture response headers/correlation IDs and check whether stack-trace/debug leakage appears under variant payloads.
